verses.text: The narrative pauses Joseph's story to follow Judah. He leaves his brothers and stays with an Adullamite friend. There he marries a Canaanite woman, the daughter of Shua. Judah is making his life outside the covenant family.

The narrative pauses Joseph's story to follow Judah. He leaves his brothers and stays with an Adullamite friend. There he marries a Canaanite woman, the daughter of Shua. Judah is making his life outside the covenant family.
She bears him three sons — Er, Onan, and Shelah. Three boys whose names Genesis records but does not celebrate. The covenant line is being grown by a Canaanite woman in a Canaanite town.
She bears him three sons — Er, Onan, and Shelah. Three boys whose names Genesis records but does not celebrate. The covenant line is being grown by a Canaanite woman in a Canaanite town.
She bears him three sons — Er, Onan, and Shelah. Three boys whose names Genesis records but does not celebrate. The covenant line is being grown by a Canaanite woman in a Canaanite town.

Judah takes a wife for Er, his firstborn — her name is Tamar. But Er was wicked in the eyes of the LORD, and the LORD slew him. Genesis doesn't tell us how he was wicked. Tamar is a widow before her story has begun.

Judah takes a wife for Er, his firstborn — her name is Tamar. But Er was wicked in the eyes of the LORD, and the LORD slew him. Genesis doesn't tell us how he was wicked. Tamar is a widow before her story has begun.

Judah tells his second son Onan: marry your brother's widow and give him an heir — the levirate duty. But Onan knew the child would not be counted his, so whenever he went in to Tamar he spilled his seed on the ground. He defrauded her of her future, and the LORD slew him too.

Judah tells his second son Onan: marry your brother's widow and give him an heir — the levirate duty. But Onan knew the child would not be counted his, so whenever he went in to Tamar he spilled his seed on the ground. He defrauded her of her future, and the LORD slew him too.

Judah tells his second son Onan: marry your brother's widow and give him an heir — the levirate duty. But Onan knew the child would not be counted his, so whenever he went in to Tamar he spilled his seed on the ground. He defrauded her of her future, and the LORD slew him too.

Judah tells Tamar: stay a widow in your father's house until Shelah grows up. But really he is afraid Shelah will die too. He never intends to give her the youngest son. Tamar is being quietly written out of the family.
After a long time Judah's wife dies. When the mourning is over, Judah goes up to Timnah for the sheep-shearing — a season of feasts. He has moved on. He has forgotten her.

Tamar hears Judah is coming. She takes off her widow's clothes, puts on a veil, and sits at the entrance to Enaim, on the road. She has seen Shelah grow up while she remained a widow forever — and she takes her future into her own hands.

Tamar hears Judah is coming. She takes off her widow's clothes, puts on a veil, and sits at the entrance to Enaim, on the road. She has seen Shelah grow up while she remained a widow forever — and she takes her future into her own hands.

Judah sees her and assumes she is a roadside woman because of the veil. He turns aside: let me come in to you. What will you give me? A young goat from my flock. Will you give me a pledge until you send it?

Judah sees her and assumes she is a roadside woman because of the veil. He turns aside: let me come in to you. What will you give me? A young goat from my flock. Will you give me a pledge until you send it?

Judah sees her and assumes she is a roadside woman because of the veil. He turns aside: let me come in to you. What will you give me? A young goat from my flock. Will you give me a pledge until you send it?

What pledge? she asks. Your signet, your cord, your staff — the three things that identify you. He gives them and goes in to her. She conceives. The patriarch hands his own identity to a stranger on the road.
Afterwards she rises, removes the veil, and puts her widow's clothes back on. Tamar disappears back into the silence assigned to her. But not for long.

Judah sends his friend with the goat to retrieve the pledge. The friend asks the men of the place: where is the prostitute who sat here? There has been no prostitute here, they answer. Judah lets her keep the pledge: let her keep it, lest we be shamed. He cares more about his reputation than about justice.

Judah lets her keep the pledge: let her keep it, lest we be shamed. He cares more about his reputation than about justice.

Judah lets her keep the pledge: let her keep it, lest we be shamed. He cares more about his reputation than about justice.

Judah sends his friend with the goat to retrieve the pledge. The friend asks the men of the place: where is the prostitute who sat here? There has been no prostitute here, they answer. Judah lets her keep the pledge: let her keep it, lest we be shamed. He cares more about his reputation than about justice.

Three months later, Judah hears Tamar is pregnant. Bring her out and let her be burned. The sentence is brutal. The man who slept with a prostitute on the road has no problem ordering his daughter-in-law's death.

As she is brought out, Tamar sends a quiet message to her father-in-law: by the man whose these are, I am pregnant. See whose signet and cord and staff these are. She speaks softly, but the evidence speaks loudly.

Judah recognizes them: she is more righteous than I — because I did not give her to Shelah. He doesn't excuse her actions or his. He simply names the truth. The judge becomes the judged. He never went in to her again.
Tamar's labor begins, and there are twins. As one puts out a hand, the midwife ties a scarlet thread around it: this one came out first. The smallest detail is being recorded for a reason.
Tamar's labor begins, and there are twins. As one puts out a hand, the midwife ties a scarlet thread around it: this one came out first. The smallest detail is being recorded for a reason.

But the baby pulls his hand back, and his brother comes out first. How have you broken through? she says — and names him Perez, meaning breaking through. The other one she names Zerah, meaning scarlet. Perez will be the ancestor of David, and of Jesus. The Canaanite widow is great-grandmother to the line of the Messiah.

The other one she names Zerah, meaning scarlet. Perez will be the ancestor of David, and of Jesus. The Canaanite widow is great-grandmother to the line of the Messiah.
